verse_specific_anchors: adhikāra (the right/jurisdiction restricted to action) — rendered as 'your jurisdiction extends only to the action itself'
mā phaleṣu kadācana (never to fruits) — rendered as 'the fruits ... are never your jurisdiction, at any time'

mā karma-phala-hetuḥ bhūḥ (do not be the cause of the fruits) — rendered in v3.1 as 'do not regard yourself as the cause of those fruits ... To inwardly claim authorship of the result is to bind yourself as the agent of consequences you cannot control' — this is now an exact verbal match for karma-phala-hetuḥ proper

mā te saṅgaḥ astu akarmaṇi (let there be no attachment to inaction) — rendered as 'do not let this become a license for retreat ... inaction disguised as caution' — the load-bearing anchor that distinguishes 2.47 from neighboring detachment verses
verdict_reason: The analog passes the force-fit test because it carries all THREE of 2.47's distinct second-hemistich prohibitions — right-restricted-to-action, agent-of-fruit, attachment-to-inaction — and the second concrete scenario (two weeks rewriting an internal CLI as displacement of the queued schema migration) directly anchors on the akarmaṇi clause. No other Gita verse contains this exact three-clause construction. Verses like BG 2.50 (yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam) or BG 3.19 (kuru karma eva tasmāt tvam) share part of the karma-yoga theme but not the three-prohibition structure of 2.47. The analog could be confused only with BG 2.48 because 2.48 is the immediate doctrinal gloss of 2.47 (samatvaṁ yoga ucyate). Caveat: I am evaluating against training-time knowledge of nearby verses rather than receiving three randomly drawn non-chapter-2 verses as the harness intends. With three actually-random non-chapter-2 verses, the analog would not equally fit any of them.
